original_hypothesis.title: TraitForgeNft whitelist Merkle leaf encoding mismatch can lock out intended whitelisted users (case/format sensitivity)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: TraitForgeNft computes leaf as keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender)), while the provided Merkle tree generator hashes addresses with keccak256(addressString) in JS. If address normalization differs (case checksum, string vs 20-byte encoding), proofs may fail for legitimate users, causing mint DoS during whitelist phase.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a common integration bug: JS libraries often hash the ASCII string representation, while Solidity hashes the 20-byte address. Unless the generator uses the 20-byte packed address bytes, the roots won't match, and whitelist minting will be unusable.
deployment_assumptions: Merkle tree leaves are generated from address strings (ASCII) rather than 20-byte packed addresses matching Solidity's abi.encodePacked(address)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: genMerkleTreeLib.ts uses `keccak256(leaf)` where `leaf` is an address string from WHITELIST; Solidity uses `keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender))`.

proof_of_signal.pattern: leaf = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender)) vs JS keccak256(addressString) leaf generation

preconditions: Root hash set from a tree generated using a different leaf encoding than Solidity

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: TraitForgeNft.sol onlyWhitelisted modifier; scripts/genMerkleTreeLib.ts leaf hashing
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: legitimate users cannot mint; protocol revenue/launch disrupted
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No on-chain helper to compute leaf and validate against expected format; no dual-hash support

analysis_questions: Does genMerkleTreeLib hash the 20-byte address bytes or the ASCII string?
Is WHITELIST normalized (lowercase) and consistent with Solidity encoding?
Are proofs built with tree.getProof vs getHexProof (buffer format mismatches)?
validation_requirements: Recompute leaf in JS exactly as Solidity: keccak256(solidityPacked(['address'],[addr])) and compare
End-to-end test: generate proof with scripts, call mintToken, verify passes

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: No attacker needed: a leaf mismatch causes all whitelist mints to revert with 'Not whitelisted user', effectively a launch-blocking DoS until rootHash or generator is fixed.
prerequisites: Incorrect off-chain Merkle generation or inconsistent address normalization

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Medium: whitelist mint phase unusable; delays and lost revenue
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can update rootHash; may require regenerating proofs

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No automated test proving whitelist proofs generated by scripts pass on-chain

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: High revert rate on mintToken/mintWithBudget with 'Not whitelisted user'

validation_result.impact_summary: Whitelist minting is completely broken because off-chain leaf generation hashes the ASCII address string while on-chain verification hashes the 20-byte packed address.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The vulnerability stems from a mismatch in how the Merkle tree leaves are encoded and hashed between the off-chain generation script and the on-chain verification logic. In `TraitForgeNft.sol`, the `onlyWhitelisted` modifier is called with `keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender))`. The `abi.encodePacked` function encodes the `msg.sender` as a 20-byte binary address. However, in the provided deployment script `genMerkleTreeLib.ts`, the leaves are generated using `keccak256(leaf)`, where `leaf` is a string representation of the address (e.g., `'0x123...'`). The `keccak256` library in JavaScript, when passed a string, hashes the 42-character ASCII string rather than the 20-byte binary value. Consequently, the leaf hashes generated off-chain will never match the leaf hashes computed on-chain, causing `MerkleProof.verify` to always return `false`. This results in a complete Denial of Service (DoS) for all legitimate users attempting to mint during the whitelist phase.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code evidence clearly shows the mismatch. `TraitForgeNft.sol` uses `keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender))` which produces a hash of the 20-byte address. `genMerkleTreeLib.ts` maps over an array of strings and calls `keccak256(leaf)`, which produces a hash of the ASCII string. Because the inputs to the hash function differ in length and content, the resulting hashes and the final Merkle root will be incompatible with the on-chain verification.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet:
function mintToken(
    bytes32[] calldata proof
  )
    public
    payable
    whenNotPaused
    nonReentrant
    onlyWhitelisted(proof, keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender)))
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The leaf is computed on-chain by hashing the 20-byte binary representation of the caller's address.
The leaf is computed off-chain by hashing the 42-character ASCII string representation of the address, creating a fundamental mismatch with the on-chain logic.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When a whitelisted user calls `mintToken` with their valid off-chain proof, the `onlyWhitelisted` modifier computes the leaf as `keccak256(abi.encodePacked(msg.sender))`. It then calls `MerkleProof.verify`. Because the `rootHash` was constructed using ASCII string hashes, the binary address hash will not match any leaf in the tree. The verification fails, and the transaction reverts with 'Not whitelisted user'.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Merkle tree leaves are generated from address strings (ASCII) rather than 20-byte packed addresses matching Solidity's abi.encodePacked(address)
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The script `genMerkleTreeLib.ts` explicitly uses `keccak256(leaf)` where `leaf` is a string from the `address` array. The `keccak256` npm package hashes the string characters rather than parsing it as a hex buffer.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: No attacker is required to exploit this. It is an integration defect that naturally causes a Denial of Service. When the protocol launches and the whitelist phase begins, all legitimate whitelisted users will attempt to mint using the proofs provided by the project's frontend. Every single transaction will revert, rendering the whitelist phase completely unusable until the owner updates the `rootHash` with a correctly generated tree.
validation_result.impact_assessment: High impact on protocol launch and user experience. The whitelist minting phase is completely broken. This will cause a 24-hour delay in minting or require emergency intervention by the contract owner to set a new, correctly computed Merkle root.
recommended_mitigations: Update the off-chain Merkle tree generation script to hash the 20-byte binary address. Using ethers.js, this can be done via `ethers.solidityPackedKeccak256(['address'], [address])`.
Alternatively, in Node.js using the `keccak256` package, convert the hex string to a buffer before hashing: `keccak256(Buffer.from(address.replace('0x', ''), 'hex'))`.
validation_result.replication_plan:
1. Run `genMerkleTreeLib.ts` with a test address to generate a root hash and proof.
2. Deploy `TraitForgeNft` and call `setRootHash` with the generated root.
3. Attempt to call `mintToken` from the test address using the generated proof.
4. Observe the transaction reverting with 'Not whitelisted user'.
additional_testing_needed: Implement an end-to-end test that generates the Merkle tree off-chain, sets the root on-chain, and successfully executes a whitelist mint to ensure encoding formats match.
